beats.prompt: Warm-up: you're waiting for HH and have a lone H. Match each next flip to what it does to your progress.

feedback.correct: Right — a T after a lone H throws the progress away. Memory matters.
hints: A T breaks the HH streak.

beats.prompt: You have $2. Each flip: heads +$1, tails −$1, until you hit $4 or $0. The coin is fair — what's the chance you go broke first?

options: 0% — a fair coin keeps me safe

0% — a fair coin keeps me safe.note: A fair coin doesn't mean safe — you can absolutely hit $0 first. Let's see how often.

About 50%.note: Good read — starting halfway between $0 and $4, it's a 50/50 by symmetry.

More than 50%.note: Close in spirit, but from the exact middle it's symmetric — 50/50.

hints: No wrong guess yet — commit and we'll walk it.
You start exactly halfway between the walls.

beats.prompt: Read why you're never 'due' — the coin has no memory — then continue.

interaction.title: You're never "due"
interaction.body: After three losses in a row it feels like a win is overdue. It isn't — each flip is still 50/50, with no memory of the streak. The coin doesn't owe you anything.

feedback.correct: Each flip is independent — no debt, no "due."
hints: Streaks don't tilt the next flip.

beats.prompt: Press Run to watch one walk from $2, then run the batch and see the full outcome spread.

hero.structuralReadout: From $2 on a fair coin: reach $4 half the time, go broke half the time.

feedback.correct: One token, random steps, until it hits a wall.

beats.prompt: Match each amount to what happens when the walk reaches it.

feedback.correct: Right — $0 and $4 absorb the walk. The game ends there.
hints: The walk stops at a wall.

beats.prompt: Read the difference between chance and duration for the same walk, then continue.

interaction.title: Chance vs how long
interaction.body: Run many walks. The CHANCE you reach $4 is the share of walks that end at the top. The DURATION is the average number of flips before a wall. Same walk, two different things to measure.

feedback.correct: Chance = share that win; duration = average flips.
hints: One is a fraction of walks.

beats.prompt: Tap the tiles into the slots to build the chance-of-reaching-$4 rule for $2. From $2, half the time you step to $3, half to $1 — no per-flip cost for a chance.

rows.note: $0 — broke. Chance of reaching $4 from here = 0.
$4 — the goal. Chance of reaching $4 from here = 1.

copy.legendLead: Pᵢ = the chance you reach $4 starting from $i.

const-generic: A chance has no per-flip cost — there's no "+1" here.
The first slot is 0: a probability just splits between the two neighbors.
wrong-var-generic: From $2, one flip goes up to $3 and one goes down to $1.
Each branch lands on a neighbor: $3 (heads) or $1 (tails).

feedback.correct: P₂ = ½·P₃ + ½·P₁ — no +1, just a split.
hints: Start the row with the per-flip cost — but a chance has none.
Then split: half to the dollar above, half to the dollar below.

beats.prompt: Tap the tiles into the slots to build the average-flips rule for $2. Same split as before — but this time every flip costs 1, so the +1 comes back.

rows.note: $0 — broke. The game is already over: 0 more flips.
$4 — the goal. Already there: 0 more flips.

copy.legendLead: Dᵢ = the average number of flips from $i until a wall.

const-zero: Zero would make this free — but you still flip a coin to move.
Every interior step costs 1 flip, so the first slot is 1, not 0.
const-generic: The first slot is the flat cost every flip pays.
Each visit costs exactly 1 flip before the coin splits — set 1.
wrong-var-generic: From $2, one flip goes up to $3 and one goes down to $1.
Each branch lands on a neighbor: $3 (heads) or $1 (tails).

feedback.correct: D₂ = 1 + ½·D₃ + ½·D₁ — the +1 is the flip you just spent.
hints: What does every flip cost, win or lose?
Same split as before, but a chance had no cost and a duration does.

beats.prompt: Solve both systems for the start at $2. Type the chance of reaching $4 and the average number of flips.

fields.label: Average flips from $2 until a wall

feedback.correct: Exactly — P₂ = ½ and D₂ = 4. Chance: with P₀ = 0 and P₄ = 1, the rule P₂ = ½·P₃ + ½·P₁ makes each dollar the average of its neighbors, so the chances climb in a straight line, Pᵢ = i/4 — at $2 that's 2/4 = ½, the symmetry you spotted up top. Duration: D₂ = 1 + ½·D₃ + ½·D₁ with D₀ = D₄ = 0 solves to Dᵢ = i·(4−i), the two distances to the walls multiplied — at $2 that's 2·(4−2) = 4 flips, the longest wait since $2 sits farthest from both walls.
hints: By symmetry the chance is ½.

beats.prompt: Use the +/− buttons to set the coin 60/40 against you, then press Run and watch how often you go broke.

hero.structuralReadout: With a 60/40 edge against you, from $2 you go broke about 69 of every 100 walks.

beats.interviewNote: This is the canonical first-passage / risk-of-ruin result: P_i = i/N when fair, and (1−r^i)/(1−r^N) with r = q/p when biased. It's why a small house edge compounds into near-certain ruin against a finite bankroll.

feedback.correct: A small edge is catastrophic — and the average walk is short, but a few run long.
hints: Lower the bias and watch the reach curve sag.
The landscape warps into a cliff.

beats.prompt: Mastery challenge: starting at $3, what is your chance of reaching $10 before going broke (as a fraction), and how many flips will the game last on average (a whole number)?

interaction.scenario: Each fair flip pays \(+\$1\) on heads, \(-\$1\) on tails. You stop at \(\$10\) (goal) or \(\$0\) (broke).

fields.label: Average flips until the game ends

feedback.correct: \(P_3 = \tfrac{3}{10}\) and the game lasts \(21\) flips on average. For a fair walk between \(\$0\) and \(\$N\) starting at \(\$i\), two closed-form results hold: \[ P_i = \tfrac{i}{N}, \] \[ D_i = i \cdot (N - i). \] With \(i = 3\), \(N = 10\): \(P_3 = \tfrac{3}{10}\), and \(D_3 = 3 \cdot (10 - 3) = 3 \cdot 7 = 21\). The win chance climbs in a straight line from \(\$0\) to \(\$10\) — a fair coin is no safety net for a short stack. Duration equals the product of the two wall-distances, peaking at the midpoint.
hints: \(\$3\) out of \(\$10\) is not the midpoint, so the win chance is not \(\tfrac{1}{2}\). Both formulas take any start \(i\) and any \(N\).
Win chance is straight-line: \(P_i = \tfrac{i}{N}\). Expected length is the product of the two wall-distances: \(D_i = i \cdot (N - i)\).

beats.prompt: Reveal the recap cards to review what you proved about Gambler's Ruin.

feedback.correct: Fair still ruins the shorter stack; a tiny edge makes ruin near-certain.
